channel_name: This Week in Startups · Jason Calacanis
video_title: $100T is managed by “human duct tape” | E2308
gist: Hanover Park CEO Chris Halasik explains how his AI-native fund administration startup grew AUM on its platform from $1B to $20B in 15 months by replacing 'human duct tape' (QuickBooks/Bill.com/Excel-based fund admin) with agent-built ERPs and bps-of-AUM pricing; the second half revisits a 2020 Figma/Dylan Field clip and Jason Calacanis's Mahalo/Google Panda story to trace SaaS pricing's evolution toward usage- and outcome-based models and Google's search-platform power.

insights.claim: Hanover Park's thesis is that the $100 trillion global fund industry runs on 'human duct tape' — legacy fund administrators staffed by accountants who buy QuickBooks, Bill.com and Excel and often withhold LPs' and GPs' own fund data from them, creating an opening for an AI-native replacement.
insights.speaker: Chris Halasik, CEO of Hanover Park
insights.so_what: Frames legacy fund administration as a large, low-tech-penetration incumbent market ripe for AI-native disruption — a sourcing lens for investors screening fintech infrastructure deals.
insights.quote: It's human duct tape. Legacy services businesses that are running the entire backbone of these hundred trillion of global assets. They're buying QuickBooks. They're buying Bill.com. They're buying Excel.

insights.claim: In 2024, Hanover Park made the contrarian bet that 'B2B SaaS was dead' and chose to build an AI-native services company that owns the end-to-end outcome (an ERP for funds) rather than a point tool that would get commoditized by general models like Claude and ChatGPT.
insights.speaker: Chris Halasik, CEO of Hanover Park
insights.so_what: Signals a broader startup strategy pattern investors are seeing: betting on vertical, outcome-owning AI-services businesses instead of thin SaaS wrappers vulnerable to model commoditization.
insights.quote: B2B SAS was dead. But everyone's like, "You're insane. You want to go build financial info from the most complex investment firms in the entire world and you've never done this before.

insights.claim: Hanover Park's own investors initially laughed at the plan to build a general ledger/ERP for complex investment firms from scratch, viewing it as an unrealistically hard problem for a first-time team to tackle.
insights.speaker: Chris Halasik, CEO of Hanover Park
insights.so_what: Illustrates that consensus investor skepticism toward 'too hard, too unsexy' infrastructure bets can precede outsized outcomes when a team solves the core technical bottleneck.

insights.claim: Hanover Park's platform AUM grew from roughly $1 billion to $15 billion between its funding rounds and reached about $20 billion within approximately 15 months, alongside a $27 million raise in March.
insights.speaker: host (Alex Wilhelm), citing prior conversations with Chris Halasik
insights.so_what: A 20x AUM growth rate in ~15 months is a hypergrowth signal for a fintech infrastructure company still capturing well under 1% of a $100T total addressable market.
insights.quote: You've gone from 1 to 20 in essentially 15 months.

insights.claim: Hanover Park prices as basis points off assets under management (bips of AUM) in an all-in-one bundle rather than traditional SaaS subscriptions or the industry-standard per-transaction/hourly billing (e.g., extra fees per capital call or distribution).
insights.speaker: Chris Halasik, CEO of Hanover Park
insights.so_what: An AUM-linked, all-in-one pricing model aligns vendor revenue with client asset growth and removes the opaque hidden-fee structure that has historically frustrated fund CFOs — a differentiator worth benchmarking against other AI-native vertical services startups.

insights.claim: Using an illustrative (not company-confirmed) estimate of 25 basis points on Hanover Park's roughly $20 billion in AUM, the host calculates approximately $50 million in annualized run-rate revenue, though Chris Halasik declined to confirm the actual bips rate or revenue.
insights.speaker: host (Alex Wilhelm)
insights.so_what: Gives investors a rough revenue-multiple framework for benchmarking AUM-based fintech infrastructure businesses, while flagging that the true take-rate is undisclosed and could be materially lower (e.g., 15 bips).
insights.quote: If Hanover Park took, for example, 25 bips off their AUM and they have 20 billion in AUM today, that's about $50 million a year in run rate.

insights.claim: Hanover Park commits to keeping each customer's fund-level data siloed and will not aggregate, deanonymize, or sell it as a cross-client data product, even though doing so could unlock a valuable data-business layer.
insights.speaker: Chris Halasik, CEO of Hanover Park
insights.so_what: A deliberate trade-off of a lucrative data-monetization angle for customer trust — worth watching for whether this policy holds as the company scales and competitors potentially pursue aggregated fund-data products.
insights.quote: I might be bucking the trend here but I'd rather them be like you know keep their data siloed.

insights.claim: Hanover Park has doubled headcount to 50 people in the past quarter and is actively hiring (including a chief-of-staff role) to sustain hypergrowth.
insights.speaker: Chris Halasik, CEO of Hanover Park
insights.so_what: Headcount doubling in a single quarter alongside 20x AUM growth signals the company is scaling operations aggressively to match demand, a useful growth-stage marker for follow-on investors.

insights.claim: Hanover Park is currently focused exclusively on closed-end funds (venture capital, private equity, private credit) and on serving asset managers, explicitly ruling out expansion into adjacent SaaS categories like corporate expense/credit-card tools (a Ramp-style business), while envisioning long-term extensibility of its ERP into other asset classes such as commodities.
insights.speaker: Chris Halasik, CEO of Hanover Park
insights.so_what: Defines both the near-term competitive moat (asset-manager-only focus, avoiding a two-front war with corporate-fintech incumbents) and the long-term expansion optionality (new asset classes) investors should track.
insights.quote: There's 100 trillion out there as you as you've said a few times. There's a there's a lot of room to run. Um, and we're focused on the asset manager for now.

themes.theme: AI Agents, Ledger Complexity & the CPA's Changing Role

insights.claim: Hanover Park onboarded a venture capital fund's roughly 20 legal entities and delivered limited-partner reporting just six days after receiving the fund's data — versus the traditional fund-administration migration timeline the CEO pegs at around 24 months.
insights.speaker: Chris Halasik, CEO of Hanover Park
insights.so_what: A ~100x reduction in migration time (24 months to 6 days) is a concrete efficiency wedge that could accelerate customer acquisition and displacement of incumbent fund administrators.
insights.quote: We took that data, we got that data, we launched their limited partners six days later.

insights.claim: Hanover Park's 'one-click migration' capability — deemed a joke by engineers internally 12 months earlier — became achievable only in the last three to six months, coinciding with access to a more capable frontier model referred to as 'Opus 4.6.'
insights.speaker: Chris Halasik, CEO of Hanover Park
insights.so_what: A direct, named example of a frontier-model capability jump unlocking a previously infeasible product feature — useful evidence for investors tracking how model upgrades translate into vertical AI product step-changes.
insights.quote: That was only that was only possible probably 3 to six months ago with like Opus 46.

insights.claim: Chris Halasik argues the remaining bottleneck to full automation in fund administration is less a raw model 'intelligence gap' and more a 'context gap' — the difficulty of capturing a given fund's idiosyncratic legal, economic, and reporting context — implying current frontier models (e.g., Opus 4.6-class) are already intelligent enough for the task.
insights.speaker: Chris Halasik, CEO of Hanover Park
insights.so_what: Reframes the AI-adoption bottleneck in vertical enterprise workflows from 'better models' to 'better context/data engineering,' a distinction investors should apply when assessing whether an AI startup's moat depends on frontier-model access or proprietary data plumbing.
insights.quote: I think that the gap is is less of a intelligence gap and more of a context gap is the way I think of it.

insights.claim: Large fund managers such as Blackstone can have hundreds of legal entities within a single fund structure, each with distinct, lawyer-drafted profit-and-loss allocation terms defined in limited partnership agreements — a level of bespoke complexity Hanover Park says is far beyond a standard single-entity system like QuickBooks.
insights.speaker: Chris Halasik, CEO of Hanover Park
insights.so_what: Explains why fund-admin software is structurally harder to build than generic SMB accounting software, supporting a thesis that a working general ledger for complex funds is a defensible, hard-to-replicate technical asset.

insights.claim: Hanover Park built 'long-horizon agents' to perform financial data cleaning at scale, ingesting up to hundreds of thousands of documents (e.g., 300,000 documents spanning 25 years of history for a fund with $20 billion in assets) and mapping them into a fund-specific data ontology before onboarding is complete.
insights.speaker: Chris Halasik, CEO of Hanover Park
insights.so_what: Document-scale data ingestion and long-horizon agent reliability is the core technical unlock behind Hanover Park's fast migrations — a proof point for the broader thesis that long-horizon agentic AI is now viable for high-stakes back-office document processing.

insights.claim: Roughly 95% of traditional fund-administration work is low-value bookkeeping verification ('did you book this journal entry correctly'); as AI automates that layer and delivers real-time rather than quarterly-delayed reporting, the CPA's role is shifting toward high-value advisory work Halasik calls being a 'consigliere to the CFO.'
insights.speaker: Chris Halasik, CEO of Hanover Park
insights.so_what: Predicts a bifurcation in fund-accounting labor: automatable routine bookkeeping shrinks while judgment-heavy advisory work persists and is repriced upward — relevant to how investors should value human-in-the-loop staffing costs in AI-native fintech services models.
insights.quote: By the way, 95% of fund admins is did you book this journal entry correctly by the way?

insights.claim: Hanover Park deliberately employs no product managers or designers, instead having engineers ship code while working directly alongside CPAs/fund accountants, who both service clients and inform product decisions.
insights.speaker: Chris Halasik, CEO of Hanover Park
insights.so_what: An unconventional org-design pattern (domain experts embedded directly with engineers, no PM/design layer) that other AI-native vertical services startups may replicate to compress the feedback loop between real edge cases and shipped product.

themes.theme: SaaS Pricing Evolution: Seats to Tokens to Outcomes

insights.claim: In a 2020 clip, Figma CEO Dylan Field said Figma considered but rejected Slack's per-active-user pricing model — despite finding that it 'incentivizes the right behaviors' — because customers/investors indicated they disliked its unpredictable, variable-cost nature; Figma instead charges only for 'editors' while 'viewers' remain free.
insights.speaker: Dylan Field, CEO of Figma (2020 clip)
insights.so_what: A concrete historical example of a company choosing pricing predictability over incentive-alignment purity — a tension still relevant when evaluating AI companies deciding between seat-based, usage-based, and outcome-based pricing.
insights.quote: We're not doing it for Figma because we've actually heard people invest that they don't like it.

insights.claim: The hosts trace SaaS monetization's historical progression — from selling boxed software, to per-seat hosted subscriptions, to active-user pricing (Slack/Figma era), to today's usage/token-based AI compute pricing — and predict the next stage will be outcome-based pricing, charging for what the AI actually delivered rather than the compute consumed.
insights.speaker: hosts (Alex Wilhelm and Lon Harris)
insights.so_what: Outcome-based pricing is flagged as the next major monetization shift for AI-native software companies — a model to watch for adoption among vertical AI startups (including Hanover Park's own AUM-linked pricing, which is itself an early outcome-adjacent model).

insights.claim: Unlike SaaS seat overages, which enterprises could typically walk back via a support email and credit, large AI token/compute spend is generally non-refundable once consumed, making runaway AI usage bills (e.g., 'our claude bill') a newly painful and irreversible budget-line item for enterprises.
insights.speaker: hosts (Alex Wilhelm and Lon Harris)
insights.so_what: Highlights a real cost-control risk in the shift to consumption-based AI pricing: enterprises face less recourse for overspend than they had under seat-based SaaS contracts, which could slow enterprise AI budget expansion or spur demand for usage-capping tools.
insights.quote: You can't email Dario and say, "Dario, can I take back that $10 million in token spend?" I didn't mean to.

themes.theme: Bottom-Up Distribution & Compressed Enterprise AI Adoption

insights.claim: Figma's early growth relied on bottom-up, product-led adoption — individual employees trialing it free or on a credit card and then advocating internally for a company-wide purchase — rather than top-down enterprise sales, with adoption further spreading as employees carried Figma with them to new jobs.
insights.speaker: Dylan Field, CEO of Figma (2020 clip)
insights.so_what: A validated distribution playbook (individual trial to organic internal advocacy to enterprise contract) that pre-dates and is now being replicated and compressed by AI tools, per the hosts' analysis.

insights.claim: The hosts argue enterprise AI-tool adoption cycles have compressed sharply relative to the SaaS era — evaluation and purchasing that once took companies months now happens in weeks or days, because organizations are in constant urgent mode to figure out what AI to adopt or cut.
insights.speaker: hosts (Alex Wilhelm and Lon Harris)
insights.so_what: Faster enterprise buying cycles for AI tools imply shorter sales cycles and faster time-to-revenue for AI-native vendors, a structural tailwind relative to legacy SaaS go-to-market timelines.

insights.claim: VCs reportedly invested in the AI notetaking startup Granola specifically because it was already spreading virally and organically inside their own portfolio companies without any top-down mandate, reinforcing that bottom-up organic usage remains a stronger adoption (and even sourcing) signal than IT-driven rollouts.
insights.speaker: hosts (Alex Wilhelm and Lon Harris)
insights.so_what: Organic, unprompted internal tool adoption inside portfolio companies is being used by VCs as a live sourcing signal for new AI investments — a pattern investors can systematize by tracking tool usage inside their own portfolios.

insights.claim: In 2020, Figma cited security/compliance requirements (e.g., completing a SOC 2 process, controls from hiring approvals to server runbooks) as a major source of enterprise sales friction, driven by customer fears that a vendor's staff — or even a foreign state actor placing an employee inside the vendor, as allegedly happened at Twitter — could access or leak sensitive data; the hosts note this same trust dynamic persists in the AI era around model-training data concerns.
insights.speaker: Dylan Field, CEO of Figma (2020 clip); hosts
insights.so_what: Security/compliance trust remains a persistent enterprise sales gating factor across both the SaaS and AI eras, and the specific fear has evolved from data leakage/inspiration to models being trained on customer data at scale.

themes.theme: Google's Platform Power & the Mahalo Cautionary Tale

insights.claim: Jason Calacanis's earlier startup Mahalo, a human-curated search alternative to Google that had reached about $10 million/year run rate, was destroyed virtually overnight when a Google algorithm update (the Panda update) reclassified it as 'web spam' and redirected roughly 80-90% of its traffic to Google itself, which later displayed scraped answers directly in a search-results 'one box.'
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis (in archival clip discussion)
insights.so_what: A first-person cautionary case study on platform dependency risk: businesses built on top of a dominant platform's traffic/ranking algorithm can be destroyed instantly by an unannounced policy or algorithm change, a risk investors should weight heavily for any startup reliant on Google, app-store, or model-provider distribution.
insights.quote: Mahalo was like my failed startup that got to 10. We were at $10 million a year... they took 80 90% of our traffic overnight.

insights.claim: Calacanis alleges Google misrepresented Mahalo's compliance (claiming 'web spam' violations despite Mahalo following Google's own stated content-length guidelines) while systematically demoting competing publishers (Mahalo, eHow, Yelp) in favor of Google's own in-results answer boxes, without offering a real opt-out — arguing this pattern constitutes a viable antitrust case against Google.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis (in archival clip discussion)
insights.so_what: A specific, named allegation of anti-competitive platform behavior that investors monitoring Google antitrust litigation risk (and analogous risk in AI-search gatekeeping) should track as a data point.
insights.quote: It was like one of the most sinister moves in the history of... when you're up against one of these big companies, they will lie to your face.

insights.claim: The host argues that whichever company ultimately wins the AI-search race will inherit outsized systemic power — becoming 'the arbiter not only of truth but of speech' — if Google alone ends up dominating both legacy search and AI-generated answers.
insights.speaker: host (Lon Harris)
insights.so_what: Frames the AI-search competitive race (Google/Gemini vs. OpenAI, Anthropic, Perplexity, etc.) as having regulatory and societal stakes beyond normal market-share competition, relevant to antitrust and AI-policy risk assessments.
insights.quote: If Google ends up uh owning the AI market as well as the historical search market, then I think they become essentially the arbiter not only of truth but of speech.

insights.claim: The hosts state that OpenAI's founding thesis, per Sam Altman and Elon Musk, was explicitly a reaction against Google's dominance — the idea that 'Google can't control this [AI]' and that a new, independent system was needed.
insights.speaker: hosts (Alex Wilhelm and Lon Harris)
insights.so_what: Ties Google's historical search-market behavior (as illustrated by the Mahalo case) directly to the competitive origin story of OpenAI, useful context for understanding the strategic rivalry framing between Google and OpenAI in AI.
insights.quote: This was honestly Sam and Elon's like open AI original spark of an idea. Like they were they were like Google can't control this.
